subject: general
title: Multi-grade teaching — Class 3 and Class 4 together
query_text: Multi-grade teaching — Class 3 and Class 4 together

immediate_steps.title: Immediate Action
steps: Divide students into two home groups — Class 3 on one side, Class 4 on the other. Give each group a different but related task on the same theme (e.g., shapes: Class 3 identifies shapes around them, Class 4 draws and labels them).
Use 'anchor activities' — give Class 4 an independent written task first, then spend focused 8–10 minutes with Class 3, then switch. This way no group is idle.
Pair one strong Class 4 student with two Class 3 students as a 'learning buddy' group for practice time — peer learning reduces your load and builds confidence in both.

classroom_activity.title: Classroom Activity — Same Story, Different Tasks
classroom_activity.activity: Choose one common theme (e.g., 'market'). Class 3 students draw a market scene and label 3 items in their language. Class 4 students write 3 sentences about what they would buy and why. After 12 minutes, one student from each group shares. Both groups hear each other — vocabulary and ideas cross over naturally.
classroom_activity.materials: Slate or notebook, chalk or pencil — nothing else needed

inclusion_strategy.title: Inclusion Strategy
inclusion_strategy.strategy: For students who are behind grade level in either class, assign the drawing/labelling task regardless of grade. This gives them a meaningful entry point without singling them out. TaRL methodology suggests grouping by ability for practice tasks — not by grade — so struggling Class 4 students can work alongside Class 3 peers without stigma.

reflection_prompt.title: Reflection
reflection_prompt.question: After trying the buddy pairing, which Class 4 student surprised you by explaining something clearly — and what does that tell you about how they learn best?

source_citation.title: Source
source_citation.reference: NCERT Class 3 & 4 Maths Mela — Preparatory Stage pedagogy (activity-based, peer group learning); TaRL methodology (Pratham) — grouping by level, not grade; NEP 2020, Section 4.6 — flexible, activity-based learning in Preparatory Stage
